questions.question: Define network topology and briefly describe any two common topologies used in LANs, highlighting one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Definition of network topology: arrangement of nodes and connections.
Description of two topologies (e.g., star, bus, ring, mesh, tree).
Advantage and disadvantage for each (e.g., star: easy management, dependency on hub; bus: simple, limited length).

questions.question: List the seven layers of the OSI model and the four layers of the TCP/IP model, and explain how the OSI layers correspond to the TCP/IP layers.
keyPoints: OSI: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.
Mapping: OSI Application/Presentation/Session → TCP/IP Application; OSI Transport → TCP/IP Transport; OSI Network → TCP/IP Internet; OSI Data Link/Physical → TCP/IP Link.

questions.question: Explain the process of data encapsulation and de-encapsulation as data moves from the application layer down to the physical layer in the OSI model, naming the PDUs at each stage. Also, describe the role of the main protocols at the transport and internet layers (e.g., TCP, UDP, IP) in ensuring reliable delivery.
keyPoints: Encapsulation steps: Application data → Segment (Transport) → Packet (Network) → Frame (Data Link) → Bits (Physical).
PDU names for each layer and the headers added at each stage.
TCP vs UDP: connection-oriented reliability vs best‑effort, flow control, congestion control.
IP: logical addressing, routing, fragmentation and reassembly.

questions.question: Discuss the three major categories of network scope (LAN, MAN, WAN) and evaluate the factors that influence the choice of network topology for an organization. Compare star, mesh, and ring topologies in terms of performance, scalability, fault tolerance, and cost, providing a practical scenario for each.
keyPoints: LAN, MAN, WAN definitions and typical geographic ranges.
Factors: bandwidth requirements, cost, ease of installation, maintenance, scalability.
Star: central hub/switch, easy management, single point of failure; good for office LANs.
Mesh: high redundancy, robust, expensive; suitable for critical communications (e.g., data centers).
Ring: token‑passing, predictable latency, vulnerable to link failure; used in some FDDI or legacy networks.
